agent1_snippets.title: Histology, Extracellular Vesicles -- Clinical Significance
agent1_snippets.content: EVs serve as vaccines in different ways. One example is the direct utilization of microbe-derived EVs as vaccines, which were proven to be effective in protecting against some bacterial diseases. EVs derived from the outer membrane of the gram-negative Neisseria meningitides, also called outer membrane vesicles (OMVs), are currently used as a vaccine against meningitis that is already licensed by several countries all over the world. Another way to use EVs as vaccines is to load microbial surface antigens on EV membranes. This method gives the vaccine higher potency than when using the soluble form of the antigen. Also, the EVs are efficient carriers for DNA and RNA vaccines. They protect the nucleic acid and increase the vaccine potency. Additionally, they are effective adjuvants for different types of vaccines. A unique advantage of the EVs is their potential to carry more than one component of the vaccine, giving a chance for higher potency. [39]

agent1_snippets.content: On the other hand, EVs play essential roles in the recognition and destruction of the microbes by the immune system. Different components of the innate and the adaptive immune systems communicate EVs among each other to orchestrate their actions aiming at combating the invaders. This process involves EVs from several sources, including both the pathogen and the host immune and non-immune cells. Besides, the immune system utilizes these EVs as direct tools in their war against these attackers. For example, decoy exosomes carry molecules that neutralize bacterial toxins like diphtheria toxins and toxins released by Staphylococcus aureus. [26]

agent1_snippets.title: Bacterial Endophthalmitis -- Treatment / Management -- Role of Vitrectomy
agent1_snippets.content: The Endophthalmitis Vitrectomy Study (EVS) led to increased use of vitrectomy for therapeutic purposes in endophthalmitis. Early vitrectomy improved visual acuity outcomes for patients with hand motion or light perception deficits. The postulated mechanisms underpinning the efficacy of vitrectomy in such scenarios include a hypothesized breach in the blood–ocular barrier, potentially enhancing the intraocular penetration of systemic antibiotics. A synergistic approach of immediate intravitreal antibiotics coupled with PPV is recommended for endophthalmitis secondary to ocular trauma with retained intraocular foreign bodies (IOFBs). This strategy, including timely foreign body removal, has been associated with significant visual recovery in most cases, notwithstanding a subset of patients who might experience visible decline after these interventions. [63]

agent1_snippets.title: Meningococcal Vaccine -- Mechanism of Action
agent1_snippets.content: A new era of vaccinology and nanotechnology entered the field of meningococcal vaccines, which facilitate the development of novel meningococcal vaccine formulations that mimic conjugation effects by using albumin-based nanoparticles encapsulated into spherical shaped micro and nanoparticles that biologically mimics N. meningitis bacteria. This way of presenting the vaccine into the body makes the immune system deal with it as an invader but without causing disease. This spherical albumin-based nanoparticle capsule contains meningococcal CPS polymers in a biodegradable matrix that slowly releases antigens. Upon contact with antigen-presenting cells as dendritic cells and macrophages, the nanoparticles will induce phagocytosis and then trigger a respiratory burst that leads to reactive oxygen species (ROS) released in huge amounts. That not only triggers the oxidative killing of invading pathogens but also potentiates a second messenger that enhances adaptive immunity. [17]

agent1_snippets.content: Intracellular dissemination occurs during the asymptomatic incubation period of enteric fever and is of primary importance in its pathogenesis. [6] Intracellular dissemination occurs via CD18 cells of the reticuloendothelial system, including macrophages, dendritic cells, polymorphonuclear monocytes, and phagocytes. [4] Invasive Salmonellae can live intracellularly by forming a modified phagosome that does not allow normal fusion with the cell's phagocyte oxidase complex. Although a triple incorrectly linked daptomycin to aminoglycoside antibacterials, the correct classification places daptomycin in a separate antibiotic family; it is a lipopeptide antibacterial that exerts its effect by inserting into bacterial cell membranes and causing rapid depolarization, leading to cell death. agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 881
agent2_snippets.content: Prophylactic vaccinations and inoculations represent a broad category of therapeutic interventions that encompass immunization, injections, and perfusion. Within this overarching group, human vaccination is a specific subclass, and it further includes distinct forms such as immunization for autoimmune disease and passive immunization (التمنيع السلبي). Passive immunization, in turn, comprises interventions like the injection of RhD immune globulin, which is recognized as a type of passive immunization. Additionally, prophylactic vaccination or inoculation against certain bacterial diseases is classified both as a therapeutic intervention (under immunization, injections, and perfusion) and as a component of the larger prophylactic vaccinations and inoculations category. This hierarchical organization highlights how various vaccination and immunization strategies are interrelated, ranging from general preventive measures to specialized applications for autoimmune conditions and passive immune protection.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 2 -- Community 650
agent2_snippets.content: Intestinal antibiotics encompass agents such as vancomycin and fidaxomicin, both of which are employed in the treatment of pseudomembranous colitis (Clostridioides difficile infection). Vancomycin belongs to the glycopeptide class of antibacterials, a group that also includes the related lipoglycopeptide telavancin. Fidaxomicin, another member of the intestinal antibiotic category, is specifically indicated for managing C. difficile–associated pseudomembranous colitis. Together, these glycopeptide‑derived and related antibiotics provide targeted therapeutic options for patients with severe colonic infections, highlighting the importance of classifying antibiotics by their spectrum and mechanism to guide effective patient care.
